speaker: interviewee
text: --Issac Hacker. They are both-- both born in Schwarzenbach, Wiener-- by Wiener Neustdat.

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- in-- in Austria?

speaker: interviewee
text: Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know the-- the-- the year?

speaker: interviewee
text: The date? I don't know exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, Samuel Hacker. The picture was taken in Schwarzenbach, Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: The year-- probably 18-- 1895-- 18-- I don't know exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, on the right side, there's a-- there's another picture. Now, who's that person there?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is my father, in the Austrian army, World War I, probably 1918, 1919 taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was taken where, that picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: But where, when, what country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Austria, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Austrian army.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, Adele Riegler, born in Lackenbach, Austria. The picture was taken during Hitler time, 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Vienna, Austria. This picture was taken in 1942, in April, when on my bar mitzvah; on that right side, Leo Hacker, my brother; on the left side, me, Ernst Hacker.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Austria-- Vienna.

speaker: interviewer
text: Vienna, good.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture, Walter Hacker on the left side; on the right side, me in Buchenwald, May, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Walter is your cousin? Walter is your cousin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Walter, my cousin, that we went through the concentration camps together.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Buchenwald, 18 of May. I am on the left side, on the corner--

speaker: interviewer
text: With a white shirt?

speaker: interviewee
text: --four--

speaker: interviewer
text: With a white shirt?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the white shirt. And four people to right is Walter, my cousin. It was a Jewish holiday. And from the American Army, the rabbi came and prayed with us. Buchenwald, 1945. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Great.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left side is my wife, Menora, her maiden name [NON-ENGLISH], on our wedding. It was in December 26, 1950, in Haifa, Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Perfect.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture, on the left side is my oldest son-- on the-- on the bottom row, Ron Hacker; in the middle Adi, my youngest; on the right, Tamir Hacker, the middle one; on the left, on the top, is my wife, Menora.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the picture was made in Los Angeles, in the 1990.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you are next to your wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm next to my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your name is Ernst Hacker.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my name is Ernst Yitzhak Hacker.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Ernst, here you are with all your family. And if you'd be very kind to-- who are you holding in your arm, who's sitting next to you, and all the people behind you? Please tell me their names.

speaker: interviewee
text: I hold in my hand, my sweet Haley Ester Hacker. Next to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Lauren Hacker.

speaker: interviewee
text: Lauren Hacker. Next to me is Menora, my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's your wife, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: My dear wife. On the corner, Adi, my youngest son-- Adi Samuel. He has my father's name. Terri, my daughter-in-law-- [NON-ENGLISH], her name. And here is my little Andrew Leo Samuel Hacker--

speaker: interviewee
text: Your grandson.

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandson.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And next to him, his father, Tamir-- Tom Hacker; and my oldest son, Ron Hacker.

speaker: interviewer
text: You have a lovely family. I want to ask you, Menora-- why did it take him so long to do what he's doing today?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you-- are you happy that he finally--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --just came out and said, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm gonna leave a testimony?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I am. It happened about three, four years ago. It started going out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made him want to talk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the-- the meeting we had with all the boys, the 90 boys in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean the Birkenau boys?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the Birkenau boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And since then he started opening.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you think, Ron? What do you think of your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I think that they had a documentary about three, four years ago, from a European company. And that company-- it was very difficult for my dad. But that kind of was the opening of his testimonial. And when that aired in European television, it-- it brought into his life an old friend of his who he hadn't met in many years. And I think that when he got together with him, which was a-- a fluke, because he had seen my father on TV. And when they reunited, I think that made it easy. Because they came out kind of together. And-- and he helped my father along with-- this is Helmut, who's like my father's best friend and-- and brother. So I think that may have been one of the-- one of the main causes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what you think of your father now that-- now that he's coming out in the open and-- and talk about it, and-- do you feel like he's leaving you a legacy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I think so. I'm learning things from the past. I was also with him over there, three, four years ago. And I've learned quite a bit. And I feel that I have something to pass on when I have kids and family of my own.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's your turn now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Well, I think that, you know, my dad-- this-- probably the most important thing, besides that the horrendous thing that's happened, is the fear that-- he's so family oriented. And he's always told us the stories-- my brothers, my two brothers. He's told us the stories when we were 10 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it's not like he's kept it inside, and shunned it away, and didn't want to have it come out. He's told us the stories. And when the Holocaust was shown 20 years ago, he told us it's even worse than this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- so it's not like he-- he's kept it away. But I think that's something that's real positive, which I want him to do, is to try to tell the story. Because I think that all Holocaust survivors-- their biggest fear is that it can happen again. It can happen again to their kids, or to their grandkids. And the biggest fear is that-- and I-- this is why I think this is healthy for him. Because every time he does something like this, it's going to let him, inside, believe that this will help it not happening again. And so to me, I'm hoping that not only does he do this, but he goes out in person, like you do, and gives seminars at temples, and at schools, and tries to tell the story, so that people-- so it's just not one Friday night that someone goes to a seminar, but it's a continuous basis. And I think it lifts a load, a responsibility, a burden on his shoulder, that-- that will help him enjoy the rest of his life in-- in a way that-- that is more enjoyable. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that your father is a singer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I know that he's an-- an entertainer.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're gonna find out when you see this tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I've seen him sing at bar mitzvahs. And I've seen him sing at weddings. And I think he likes to entertain people. And I think he likes to make sure that people have a good time. He's a great host at parties. And, you know, I-- it just-- it's hard to imagine what all of you guys went through when you were kids. And I just-- I'm happy that-- that I'm lucky-- that-- that-- that we're in a position that it-- it didn't happen to us. But I also know the responsibility that survivors' children should have in making sure that this doesn't happen, you know, 50 years from now, 100 years from now, to Jews, or any other race.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's very well said. That-- I'm sure you're very proud of your family. And I thank you very, very much for being here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thanks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you so much.
